title: US-Iran Ceasefire Collapses as Hormuz Traffic Dries Up, Ukraine Chokes Russia's Sea Lines, IMF Cuts Global Growth
subhead: Trump declares the June 17 MoU 'over' after two nights of US strikes on Iran. Hormuz transits have fallen to 23 ships per day. Ukraine's drone campaign has hit 25+ Russian vessels in four days. The IMF slashes its 2026 growth forecast to 3%, citing the Iran war's energy shock.

sections.heading: US-Iran Ceasefire Unravels as Strikes Continue
sections.body:
The US and Iran traded strikes for a second consecutive night into Thursday, marking the most serious breakdown of the June 17 memorandum of understanding. US Central Command said it struck 90 Iranian military targets — including air defense systems and coastal logistics infrastructure — in what it described as an effort to degrade Iran's ability to threaten commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz. Iran's health ministry reported 14 people killed and 78 injured across five provinces.

President Trump told reporters on Wednesday that the ceasefire agreement was now 'over,' adding: 'I don't want to deal with them anymore, they're scum.' Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi responded that Iran answers 'not with vulgarity, but with action.' Iran's parliamentary speaker and chief negotiator Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf declared that the Strait of Hormuz would only open under Iranian arrangements, not 'American threats.'

Later on Thursday, explosions were reported near the southern Iranian port of Konarak, though a US defense official told the BBC the US military had not carried out any strikes in Iran in recent hours, raising the possibility of Israeli involvement. Iranian state-linked media also reported strikes on sites in Kuwait, Jordan, and Iraq, and Gulf states reported missile interceptions and explosions in Manama, Bahrain.

The core dispute is over the Strait of Hormuz. Iran insists all commercial traffic must use its designated northern route and pay transit fees. The US and its allies have pushed for an Omani-route alternative. Three commercial ships using the Omani route were attacked earlier this week, and Iran's Revolutionary Guards warned that 'any interference in determining shipping routes' would 'provoke a crushing response.'
sections.whyItMatters: The MoU that ended the US-Israel-Iran war in mid-June lasted less than a month. The disagreement over who controls Hormuz was always the deal's weakest link, and this week's escalation confirms that neither side's interpretation can hold without enforcement power. With Trump declaring the agreement dead and Iran retaliating against US-allied targets across the Gulf, the path back to negotiations is unclear.

sections.heading: Hormuz Traffic Collapses to Near-War Lows
sections.body:
Maritime intelligence firm Kpler recorded just 23 ship transits through the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday — down from 47 the week before and well below the pre-war average of 130-138 daily crossings. The US-backed Omani southern route, which had peaked at 28 ships per day in late June, saw zero transits on Wednesday after three tankers were attacked earlier this week. Phil Belcher of Intertanko, the international tanker owners' association, said the number of ships using the southern route was now in 'single figures.'

The three ships attacked this week — a Qatar-owned LNG tanker, a Saudi-owned crude oil tanker, and a Liberia-flagged crude tanker — were all on or near the Omani route. Iran has repeatedly stated that only its designated route is safe, effectively forcing shippers to choose between Iranian-controlled passage and the risk of attack.

Brent crude futures surged as much as 7 percent on Wednesday, topping $79 a barrel at one point, before settling at $78.76. The US Strategic Petroleum Reserve fell to 319.5 million barrels in the week ending July 3 — its lowest level since 1983 — after drawdowns that began in March. Trump acknowledged to reporters that 'anytime we strike Iran, oil prices go up.'
sections.whyItMatters: Hormuz handles roughly one-fifth of global oil and gas supply. The near-total abandonment of the Omani route means Iran is effectively dictating terms of passage through one of the world's most critical waterways, and neither US military escorts nor the MoU's language has been able to change that. The SPR is at its lowest since the Reagan era, and sustained disruption would squeeze global energy markets with limited buffer.

sections.heading: Ukraine's Naval Drone Campaign Chokes Russian Supply Lines
sections.body:
Ukraine's drone forces have struck at least 25 Russian ships over the past four days in the Sea of Azov, according to drone force commander Robert Brovdi. Ukraine's general staff claims 36 vessels hit, most belonging to Russia's 'shadow fleet' of commercial oil tankers. The governor of Russia's Rostov region confirmed two tankers were attacked in Taganrog Bay and were still burning on Thursday. Satellite imagery confirmed a large plume of smoke from a vessel off the Crimean coast that had been burning since July 6.

The strikes coincide with Ukraine's continued attacks on Russian oil refineries and depots, with President Zelensky citing hits in Tver, Stavropol, and Rostov regions. Russia has banned diesel exports and over 90 percent of its regions are now experiencing fuel rationing or shortages. Putin had recently promised to secure Crimea's monthly fuel needs of 70,000 tons by increasing land and sea deliveries — exactly the supply lines Ukraine is now targeting.

Russian military bloggers have openly acknowledged the severity of the attacks. The 'Military Informant' Telegram channel called the tankers a 'shooting gallery' for Ukrainian drone operators, while Rybar noted that the Black Sea Fleet had 'shut itself in at Novorossiysk.' Trump, meeting Zelensky at the NATO summit in Ankara, described the drone campaign as 'an escalation' but added it was 'also an escalation that can help lead to an end.'
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine has moved from targeting Russia's land corridor to Crimea to attacking its maritime supply routes as well — a 'logistics lockdown' strategy that combines naval drones with refinery strikes to squeeze Russia's fuel supply on two fronts simultaneously. With the Black Sea Fleet effectively confined to port and Russian diesel exports banned, the cumulative effect on Russia's military logistics and domestic fuel supply is becoming significant.

sections.heading: Khamenei Buried as Successor Remains Absent
sections.body:
Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was buried at the Imam Reza shrine in Mashhad on Thursday, concluding six days of funeral processions across five cities. Huge crowds filled the streets of Iran's second-largest city, waving Iranian flags and red banners symbolizing vengeance. Some carried placards calling for the death of Trump and Netanyahu, who ordered the joint strike that killed Khamenei on February 28.

The most notable absence was Khamenei's successor — his son Mojtaba, who has not been seen in public since being reportedly seriously wounded in the same attack that killed his father. The 56-year-old did not attend any of the funeral ceremonies in Tehran, Qom, or the burial in Mashhad, raising questions about who is effectively running Iran during a period of military escalation.

The Revolutionary Guards accused the US of bombing two bridges and a railway line connecting Tehran to Mashhad 'in an effort to overshadow the funeral,' though the US has not confirmed those strikes. The funeral ceremonies were intended to project unity and strength after a war that killed thousands and after mass protests in January that left thousands more dead.
sections.whyItMatters: Iran is fighting a two-front confrontation with the US while its supreme leader has not appeared in public since assuming power in March. The regime is using the funeral to project resilience, but Mojtaba Khamenei's absence during the most significant political moment since the war began is a signal — whether of physical incapacity or political instability — that matters when the question of who can negotiate on Iran's behalf is most acute.

sections.heading: IMF Cuts 2026 Growth Forecast, Citing Iran War Energy Shock
sections.body:
The International Monetary Fund cut its 2026 global growth forecast to 3 percent from 3.1 percent, citing the 'lingering effects' of the energy shock caused by the US-Israel-Iran war. The downgrade came on the same day that Brent crude surged past $79 a barrel following the latest US strikes on Iran and Trump's declaration that the ceasefire was over.

The IMF said its forecast assumes the Strait of Hormuz begins reopening in mid-July and returns to a 'pre-war state' by March 2027 — an assumption that looks increasingly tenuous given this week's events. Global inflation is expected to reach 4.7 percent this year, up from 4.1 percent in 2025. The IMF noted that two forces are pulling in opposite directions: the energy shock from the Iran war and a technology-driven investment boom, particularly in AI infrastructure.

The US is forecast to grow 2.3 percent — the fastest among major advanced economies — while the Eurozone is expected at 0.9 percent, the UK at 1 percent, and Japan at 0.6 percent. China is forecast at 4.6 percent. Market analyst Fabien Yip of IG noted that oil's brief return to near pre-war levels 'suggested markets were leaning on a best-case outcome' for the MoU, and that 'this week's re-escalation is a reminder of how fragile that assumption was.'
sections.whyItMatters: The IMF's baseline already assumed Hormuz would reopen within weeks — an assumption that this week's events have called into serious doubt. If the strait remains constrained or the MoU stays collapsed, the growth downgrade is likely the first of several. The diverging forecasts between the US (2.3%) and the Eurozone (0.9%) underscore how unevenly the energy shock is distributed, with import-dependent European economies far more exposed.

sections.heading: China Tightens Anti-Sanctions Regime, Putting Multinationals in the Crossfire
sections.body:
China has passed two new regulations since March that expand its ability to retaliate against foreign entities enforcing sanctions it considers to have 'improper extraterritorial jurisdiction.' State Council Decree No. 834, passed in March, penalizes companies that 'disrupt, undermine or discriminate against' China's supply chains. Decree No. 835, passed in April, allows fines, asset freezes, visa cancellations, and investment restrictions against entities that enforce foreign sanctions. A third law, still in draft, would allow Chinese prosecutors to bring cases against foreign organizations whose 'unlawful acts harm the country's national interests.'

In May, Beijing for the first time invoked its 2021 'blocking law' to bar Chinese companies from complying with US sanctions on Chinese oil refineries buying Iranian oil. The Ministry of Justice also invoked Decree 835 to block cooperation with an EU investigation into the Chinese security firm Nuctech. Advisory firm Trivium China summarized the situation: foreign companies will be 'increasingly caught between an American rock and a Chinese hard place.'

The moves come as the US continues to restrict China's access to advanced semiconductors and the EU launches trade probes into Chinese firms. James Hsiao of White & Case told Al Jazeera that companies are concerned about how to comply with 'opposing rules and regulations' — required under US or EU sanctions to restrict business, while facing Chinese penalties for doing so.
sections.whyItMatters: Beijing is turning its anti-sanctions framework from a rhetorical tool into an enforceable legal regime. The practical impact is that multinational companies — particularly in technology, energy, and logistics — now face real legal penalties from both sides when they comply with one jurisdiction's rules. This is no longer a theoretical compliance problem; it is an operational one that forces firms to choose between access to the US financial system and access to the Chinese market.

bottomLine: The US-Iran ceasefire that took effect on June 17 is functionally dead. Trump declared it 'over'; Iran's response has been to strike US-linked targets across three Gulf states and assert unilateral control over Hormuz, where daily ship transits have fallen from 130 to 23. Neither the military escalation nor the diplomatic collapse shows a clear off-ramp — the MoU's vague language on the strait was always the deal's weakest point, and both sides have now moved past it. Separately, Ukraine's naval drone campaign has hit 25+ Russian vessels in four days, tightening the squeeze on Russian fuel logistics at a moment when domestic shortages are already acute. The IMF's growth cut to 3% assumed Hormuz would reopen this month; that assumption now looks optimistic. And China's expanding anti-sanctions regime is forcing multinationals into an untenable position between Washington and Beijing.
